stem: A nurse palpates a patient's pulse and finds it irregular. She decides to assess the apical pulse instead. Which instrument is used to assess the apical pulse?
A nurse is assessing a 2-year-old infant's apical pulse. Where is the CORRECT anatomical location of the apical pulse for this age group?
options: 5th intercostal space at the midsternal line
Midclavicular line at the 5th intercostal space
Between the midclavicular line and anterior axillary line

stem: A patient's apical pulse is 96 bpm and the radial pulse is 82 bpm. The nurse correctly documents this finding as a:
Blood viscosity increases significantly when the haematocrit exceeds which threshold, thereby increasing blood pressure?

CASE STUDY: A 68-year-old woman is admitted after a near-syncopal episode. Her lying blood pressure is 130/82 mmHg. After assisting her to stand, her BP drops to 108/70 mmHg and her pulse rises from 72 to 94 bpm. The nurse correctly identifies this as:

CASE STUDY (continued from Q5): In assessing for orthostatic hypotension, after placing the patient supine and recording baseline vital signs, the next steps are to assist her to sit or stand, immediately recheck pulse and BP in the same sites, then repeat the pulse and BP after how many minutes?
A patient with a haemoglobin of 6 g/dL has a pulse oximetry reading of 98%. The nurse correctly interprets this result as:
options: Accurate; the patient has no respiratory compromise
Potentially misleading; the haemoglobin is fully saturated but too low to adequately supply tissues, so the patient may still be hypoxic
Critically low and requiring immediate oxygen supplementation
Invalid because the pulse oximeter cannot function with anaemia

stem: A nurse is assessing a patient's blood pressure. The cuff bladder is too narrow for the patient's arm. What effect will this have on the blood pressure reading?
options: The reading will be erroneously low
The reading will be accurate if proper technique is used
The reading will be erroneously high
The reading will show a widened pulse pressure

stem: Which breathing pattern is characterised by a gradual increase and decrease in respirations with periods of apnoea?

CASE STUDY: A 42-year-old factory worker is admitted after being rescued from a building fire. He appears conscious but his SpO2 reads 99% on room air. He has a cherry-red skin colour and complains of a severe headache. The nurse is concerned the pulse oximeter reading is unreliable. Why?
options: Smoke inhalation causes bronchoconstriction that falsely elevates SpO2
Pulse oximeters cannot discriminate between haemoglobin saturated with carbon monoxide versus oxygen, giving a falsely high reading
Activity and movement during the rescue have interfered with sensor accuracy
Impaired peripheral circulation from burns has created an inaccurate reading

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q10): A life-threatening SpO2 level using a pulse oximeter would be:
The width of a blood pressure cuff bladder should be what percentage of the circumference of the arm?
A patient's blood pressure is 158/96 mmHg on two separate clinic days. The nurse correctly classifies this as:
options: Normal blood pressure within acceptable range
White coat hypertension requiring further monitoring only

stem: Peripheral vascular resistance (PVR) increases blood pressure because:
options: Increased PVR increases blood volume in the ventricles
Increased vasoconstriction increases blood pressure; the smaller the diameter of the vessel the greater the resistance
Increased PVR reduces heart rate, increasing stroke volume per beat
Increased PVR dilates arterioles to accommodate more blood flow

stem: A nurse is preparing to measure blood pressure in a patient who has an arteriovenous fistula for renal dialysis in the left arm. What is the correct action?
options: Measure in the left arm, documenting the presence of the fistula
Use a smaller cuff size for the left arm to reduce pressure on the fistula
Avoid measuring blood pressure in that limb; use the other arm
Assess blood pressure in the thigh instead of any arm

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse receives a patient post-cardiac surgery whose peripheral pulse is weak and barely palpable. She decides to assess the apical pulse. She is aware this is also routinely done before administering which category of medications?
CASE STUDY (continued from Q16): Two nurses are assigned to assess the apical-radial pulse simultaneously. For accurate measurement, both nurses should count the pulses for how long?
Vesicular breath sounds are normal breath sounds that are best described as:
options: High-pitched, loud, harsh sounds heard over the trachea
Low-pitched, gentle sighing sounds heard over the peripheral lung
Moderately pitched blowing sounds heard between the scapulae
Continuous high-pitched squeaky sounds heard over all lung fields

stem: A patient is found to have wheeze on auscultation. The nurse understands this is caused by:
options: Air passing through fluid or mucus in any air passage
Rubbing together of inflamed pleural surfaces
Air passing through a constricted bronchus due to secretions, swelling, or tumour
Air moving through large airways creating blowing sounds

stem: A pulse pressure greater than 40 mmHg is associated with which condition?
A nurse auscultates a patient's lungs and hears a superficial grating or creaking sound during both inspiration and expiration that is not altered by coughing. She correctly identifies this as:

CASE STUDY: A 30-year-old woman with asthma is admitted during an acute exacerbation. On auscultation, the nurse hears high-pitched continuous musical sounds over all lung fields, best heard on expiration. These sounds are NOT usually altered by coughing. The nurse documents these as:
CASE STUDY (continued from Q22): The patient's SpO2 drops to 88% on room air. The nurse understands that normal SpO2 should be within which range?
Hypovolaemia increases the pulse rate in adults because:
options: Reduced blood volume stimulates the parasympathetic nervous system to slow the heart
Loss of blood from the vascular system normally increases pulse rate as the heart adjusts to increase blood pressure to compensate for blood loss
Hypovolaemia directly stimulates cardiac pacemaker cells to fire more rapidly
Reduced venous return increases end-diastolic volume and stroke volume

stem: A patient's blood pressure cannot be measured on the right arm due to burns. The nurse decides to assess on the thigh. Which statement is TRUE about thigh blood pressure measurement?
options: The thigh blood pressure is always lower than the arm and should never be used for diagnosis
Thigh blood pressure assessment is indicated when the arms cannot be used and the reading should be compared with the other thigh
Thigh blood pressure is always higher and requires a special conversion formula to interpret
Thigh blood pressure is only valid in patients under 40 years of age

stem: Respiration is controlled by respiratory centres in the medulla oblongata and the pons of the brain, as well as chemoreceptors that respond to changes in which three substances?
options: Oxygen, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen concentrations

stem: A well-trained athlete is found to have a resting heart rate of 48 bpm. The nurse correctly interprets this as:
options: Normal for a well-trained athlete due to greater cardiac size, strength, and efficiency
Abnormal bradycardia caused by vagal nerve overactivation
Tachycardia threshold not yet reached and no action needed

stem: CASE STUDY: A 55-year-old male patient tells the nurse he works night shifts and has not slept in 36 hours. His BP is 148/92 mmHg. He appears anxious and agitated. Which factor affecting blood pressure MOST likely explains the elevated reading?
options: Stress stimulating the SNS, increasing cardiac output and vasoconstriction
Decreased blood viscosity from sleep deprivation

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q28): The patient asks the nurse what is the difference between primary and secondary hypertension. The nurse correctly explains:
options: Primary hypertension has a known cause; secondary hypertension does not
Primary (essential) hypertension has no known cause; secondary hypertension has a known identifiable cause
Primary hypertension only affects the elderly; secondary hypertension affects all ages
Primary hypertension is treated with lifestyle changes only; secondary requires medication

stem: Crackles (rales) are abnormal breath sounds best described as:
options: Continuous high-pitched sounds caused by narrowed bronchi
Short interrupted crackling sounds caused by air passing through fluid or mucus; best heard on inspiration
Grating sounds caused by inflamed pleural surfaces rubbing together
Coarse low-pitched sounds caused by secretions in the trachea and bronchi

stem: Systolic blood pressure represents pressure in the arteries during which cardiac event?
A patient's blood pressure falls to 90/60 mmHg post-operatively following major abdominal surgery with significant blood loss. The nurse attributes the decline in blood pressure PRIMARILY to:
options: Reduced peripheral vascular resistance from anaesthetic agents
Decreased blood volume leading to less fluid in the vessels
Impaired cardiac pumping from surgical stress

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is auscultating the lungs of a 70-year-old COPD patient. She hears a continuous low-pitched coarse sound with a moaning or snoring quality predominantly over the trachea and bronchi, best heard on expiration. The nurse documents this as:
CASE STUDY (continued from Q33): The nurse notes the gurgles are partially cleared after the patient coughs. Which characteristic of gurgles explains this finding?
options: Gurgles are due to pleural inflammation that resolves temporarily with coughing
Gurgles may be altered by coughing because they are caused by secretions in the airways that can be partially mobilised
Gurgles are fixed airway sounds unrelated to secretion clearance
Coughing increases intrathoracic pressure and temporarily silences all breath sounds

stem: A nurse assesses pulse volume as 'bounding' in a patient. This refers to:
options: A very strong, forceful pulse felt with each beat
An absent pulse in the peripheral vessels
A pulse that alternates between strong and weak beats

stem: Diaphragmatic (abdominal) breathing involves contraction and relaxation of the diaphragm and is MOST commonly observed in which group?
An adult patient has a respiratory rate of 8 breaths per minute with shallow respirations. The nurse recognises this as hypoventilation characterised by:
options: Rapid and deep breathing with overexpansion of the lungs
Under-expansion of the lungs characterised by shallow respirations
Alternating periods of rapid breathing and apnoea
Prolonged inspiratory phase with brief expiration

stem: CASE STUDY: A 77-year-old male with congestive heart failure is admitted. He can only breathe comfortably when sitting upright and becomes severely dyspnoeic when lying flat. The nurse correctly documents this as:
CASE STUDY (continued from Q38): Auscultation of this patient's lungs reveals crackles (rales) bilaterally at the bases. The nurse understands that these crackles are MOST likely caused by:
options: Inflamed pleural surfaces rubbing together due to pleuritis
Secretions and fluid accumulation in the lower airways secondary to heart failure
Air passing through chronically constricted bronchi

stem: The carotid pulse site is specifically used during which clinical emergency?
options: Routine blood pressure assessment in hypertensive patients

stem: A patient's blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg. The pulse pressure is:

CASE STUDY: A nurse assessing a patient notes the respiratory rate is 28 breaths per minute with quick, shallow breaths. The patient appears anxious after receiving bad news. The nurse documents this breathing pattern as:
CASE STUDY (continued from Q42): The nurse understands that this patient's tachypnoea is MOST likely caused by:
options: Increased intracranial pressure from an intracranial bleed
Stress triggering the flight or fight response

stem: Arterial wall elasticity is assessed when palpating a pulse and normal arterial wall should feel:
options: Tortuous and thickened with irregular borders
Firm and bounding with high resistance to compression

stem: The popliteal pulse is used for which clinical assessment purpose?
options: Assessing circulation to the lower leg

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is about to administer digoxin 0.25 mg orally to a patient. Before administration, she assesses the apical pulse and finds it is 54 bpm. Which action is MOST appropriate?
options: Administer the digoxin as prescribed and continue monitoring
Hold the digoxin, document the finding, and notify the physician immediately, as digoxin can further lower heart rate
Administer half the dose and reassess in 30 minutes
Reassess the pulse using the radial site before deciding

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q46): The physician is notified and asks about the pulse deficit. A pulse deficit is BEST described as:
options: Any pulse rate below 60 bpm in an adult patient
The difference between apical and radial pulse rates, indicating inadequate cardiac output reaching the periphery
The absence of a radial pulse in a patient with known atrial fibrillation
A widened pulse pressure indicating arteriosclerosis

stem: The Korotkoff sounds heard during blood pressure measurement are produced by which method?
options: The pulse oximeter detecting arterial pulsations
Blood flowing through the artery as detected by the stethoscope and sphygmomanometer
The cuff bladder vibrating against the arm during inflation
The diaphragm of the stethoscope amplifying venous sounds

stem: A nurse is assessing a 5-year-old child's apical pulse. Based on age, where is the CORRECT location of the apical pulse?
options: 5th intercostal space at the midsternal line
Midclavicular line at the 5th intercostal space
Between the midsternal line and midclavicular line at the 5th intercostal space

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse places a pulse oximeter on a patient's finger who has been shivering excessively. The SpO2 reading fluctuates between 88% and 96%. The nurse recognises that this most likely reflects:
options: True hypoxaemia requiring immediate supplemental oxygen
Inaccurate readings because shivering and excessive movement at the sensor site interfere with accurate readings
Carbon monoxide poisoning creating falsely elevated saturation

stem: An excessively fast pulse rate above 100 beats per minute is called _______________, while a very slow pulse rate below 60 beats per minute is called bradycardia.
The normal adult respiratory rate is _______________ to 20 counts per minute.

CASE STUDY: Nurse Bola is preparing a patient newly started on a cardiac medication. She knows she must assess the _______________ pulse before administering any medication that affects the heart rate, and must hold the medication if the rate is outside the safe range.
Hypertension is diagnosed when blood pressure measured on two different days is greater than or equal to 140 mmHg systolic AND greater than or equal to _______________ mmHg diastolic.
Pulse pressure is the difference between the _______________ and diastolic pressures.

CASE STUDY: A student nurse asks why a blood pressure cuff must be the correct size. The preceptor explains that if the bladder is too narrow, the reading will be erroneously _______________, and if too wide, it will be erroneously low.
Normal arterial blood oxygen saturation (SpO2) ranges from 97% to 100%, and a SpO2 below _______________ % is considered life threatening.
Orthostatic hypotension is indicated by a rise in pulse of 15 to 30 beats per minute OR a drop in blood pressure of _______________ mmHg systolic or 10 mmHg diastolic upon standing.

CASE STUDY: A nurse is documenting abnormal breath sounds in a post-operative patient. She hears a grating sound in the lower anterior and lateral chest areas during both inspiration and expiration that is not relieved by coughing. She documents this as a pleural friction _______________.
An elevated blood pressure of unknown cause is called primary or _______________ hypertension.
Blood pressure is measured in millimetres of _______________ (mmHg).
The breathing pattern characterised by an abnormal pattern with groups of rapid respirations of equal depth and regular apnoea periods is called _______________ respiration.

CASE STUDY: A nurse is placing a pulse oximeter on a patient with carbon monoxide poisoning. She recognises that the reading may be falsely _______________ because pulse oximeters cannot discriminate between haemoglobin saturated with carbon monoxide versus oxygen.
The difference or disparity between the apical and radial pulse rates is called a pulse _______________.
Costal (thoracic) breathing involves the external intercostal muscles and can be observed by the upward and outward movement of the chest. It is most common among _______________.

CASE STUDY: A preceptor asks a nursing student to name the five data parameters collected when assessing the pulse. The student responds: rate, rhythm, volume, arterial wall elasticity, and presence or absence of _______________ wall equality.
Blood pressure rises with age, reaching a peak at the onset of puberty, and the elderly have an increased systolic blood pressure due to decreased elasticity of the _______________.
Diastolic pressure is the pressure in the arteries when the ventricles are at _______________.

CASE STUDY: A community nurse visits an elderly woman who reports feeling faint when she stands up quickly. To assess for orthostatic hypotension, the nurse first places her in the _______________ position, records baseline pulse and BP, then assists her to slowly stand before rechecking.
In adults, the apical pulse is located on the left side of the chest at the 4th, 5th, or 6th intercostal space, in between the midsternal line and the _______________ line.
